refinement_sections.content: Clearly define the objective of the hypothesis test: to determine if the average sales of the newly launched product exceed a specific benchmark. Specify the benchmark value, which should be based on historical sales data or industry standards.

Formulate the null hypothesis (H0) and the alternative hypothesis (H1). For instance, H0: μ ≤ benchmark (the average sales do not exceed the benchmark) and H1: μ > benchmark (the average sales exceed the benchmark).

Detail the data collection process. Specify the time frame (the past three months), the source of data (sales team reports), the sample size, and any relevant demographic information about the sales data (e.g., geographic distribution, product variations).

Choose an appropriate statistical test (e.g., one-sample t-test or z-test) based on the sample size and the distribution of the sales data. Justify the choice of test and outline the assumptions (e.g., normality, independence) required for its validity.
Determine the significance level (α), commonly set at 0.05, and explain its implications for Type I error. Discuss how this level influences the decision-making process.
Outline the steps for conducting the hypothesis test, including calculating the test statistic, determining the critical value, and making a decision based on the p-value or confidence interval.

Identify and discuss any assumptions made during the analysis (e.g., normal distribution of sales data) and potential limitations (e.g., sample bias, external market factors) that could affect the conclusions drawn from the test.

Prepare a comprehensive report that includes the methodology, findings (including test statistics, p-values, and confidence intervals), and visualizations (e.g., graphs or charts). Provide actionable recommendations based on the results of the hypothesis test.
